input.user:
Context:
[1] Brightline Logistics operates a fleet of 240 vans across the north of England. [2] The company runs out of two principal depots, with secondary holding yards in Hull and Doncaster used for occasional overflow. [3] Its main service is same-day B2B delivery for industrial parts suppliers, with a smaller line of next-day pallet movements for non-time-critical freight. [4] The company was founded in Leeds in 2014 by two former parcel-depot managers who had previously worked together at a regional courier firm. [5] In its first year of trading the business operated 12 vans and a single dispatcher, taking on customers within a 60-mile radius of the Leeds depot. [6] By 2017 the fleet had grown to 80 vans and the customer base had widened to include component suppliers serving automotive plants in the East Midlands. [7] In 2023 Brightline opened a second hub in Sheffield to handle overflow from the Leeds depot, which had been operating above its design capacity for several quarters. [8] The Sheffield hub employs 64 staff across two shifts and is laid out to allow future expansion to a third shift if demand warrants. [9] The Leeds head office houses the dispatch operations, finance, and senior leadership teams, totalling around 110 staff. [10] A field-services team of 18 engineers, based out of Leeds but operating across both depots, maintains the fleet to manufacturer schedules. [11] Brightline's customer base is concentrated in industrial parts distribution, with the largest single account representing approximately 14% of revenue. [12] The next four largest customers each contribute between 5% and 9% of revenue. [13] The remaining revenue is distributed across roughly 200 smaller accounts. [14] Average van utilisation across the fleet was 71% in 2024, with morning peaks routinely exceeding 90%. [15] The dispatch system, an in-house build, was rewritten in 2022 to support multi-depot routing. [16] Drivers are employed directly rather than contracted, a deliberate choice made by the founders to support service consistency. [17] Driver retention in 2024 was 88%, comfortably above the regional industry benchmark. [18] The company's livery, dark green with a white wordmark, was refreshed in 2023 to coincide with the Sheffield hub launch. [19] Brightline does not operate outside the UK and has no current plans to do so. [20] A sister company providing parcel locker installation, set up by one of the founders, is structured as a separate legal entity and shares no operational resources. [21] Annual revenue for 2024 was reported as £42.6M, with operating margin in the high single digits. [22] The board comprises the two founders and three non-executive directors appointed between 2019 and 2022. [23] The company has been profitable in every year since 2016. [24] No external equity capital has been raised; growth has been funded from retained earnings and asset finance against the vehicle fleet.

Question: In what year was Brightline Logistics founded?

tier_2_judge.criteria: Answer states the year 2014 concisely without speculation or extra commentary.

metadata.notes: Easy: single, directly stated founding-year fact in production-length context with neutral background sentences and no competing date distractors.

Context:
[1] Acme Co provides a workflow-automation platform aimed at mid-market operations teams. [2] The platform launched commercially in 2019 and currently serves around 4,800 paying customers worldwide. [3] Pricing is published openly on the company's website and is identical for customers in the UK and the EU, with the EU price quoted in euros at the prevailing rate. [4] The platform is sold under three plan tiers: Standard, Pro, and Enterprise. [5] The Standard plan is aimed at single-team deployments and includes the core workflow builder, up to 10 active workflows, and standard email support. [6] The Pro plan is aimed at multi-team deployments and includes unlimited workflows, role-based access control, and priority support. [7] The Enterprise plan is aimed at organisations requiring custom controls and is priced individually following a sales conversation. [8] The Acme Co Pro plan costs £89 per user per month when billed annually. [9] Discounts on Pro are available for customers committing to multi-year contracts, negotiated case by case. [10] All plans include unlimited API calls within reasonable use, governed by a separate fair-use policy. [11] The Pro plan additionally includes priority support and a 99.95% uptime SLA. [12] Standard plan customers are covered by a best-efforts uptime commitment without service credits. [13] Enterprise customers may negotiate uptime SLAs above 99.95% as part of their contract. [14] Monthly billing is available across all plans and adds a 15% surcharge to the equivalent annual rate. [15] All published prices exclude VAT, which is added at the prevailing rate at point of invoice. [16] New customers are entitled to a 14-day free trial of the Pro plan, after which their account reverts to Standard unless an upgrade is purchased. [17] The trial does not require entry of payment details. [18] Customer onboarding for Pro plan customers includes two complimentary 60-minute sessions with a customer success manager. [19] Enterprise onboarding is scoped individually and may include on-site workshops. [20] Acme publishes a public roadmap which is updated quarterly. [21] Major releases are announced via the in-product release notes and the customer mailing list. [22] Customers on any plan can submit feature requests via the in-product feedback widget. [23] Feature requests are triaged by the product team on a fortnightly cadence. [24] All plans include access to the Acme Co community forum, which is moderated by company staff during UK business hours.

Question: How much does the Acme Co Pro plan cost per user per month on annual billing?

tier_2_judge.criteria: Answer states £89 (per user per month) without inventing surcharges or conflating with the surcharge or discount language elsewhere in the context.

metadata.notes: Easy: single directly stated price in production-length pricing-page-style context with no competing price distractors for Pro.

Context:
[1] The Brightline RT-200 is a small-form-factor wired router intended for branch-office deployment. [2] It is sold both directly and through a network of authorised resellers across the UK and Ireland. [3] The product was first released in early 2023 and received a hardware revision in late 2024 that improved thermal performance under sustained load. [4] All units shipped after the revision are stamped "Rev B" on the underside of the chassis. [5] Earlier units, designated "Rev A", remain fully supported and receive the same firmware updates. [6] The chassis measures 180 mm wide by 120 mm deep by 32 mm tall and weighs approximately 540 grams. [7] The chassis is fanless and rated for operation between 0 and 45 degrees Celsius. [8] The unit draws a maximum of 18 watts under full load. [9] At idle, power draw typically falls below 6 watts. [10] The RT-200 has four gigabit Ethernet ports on the rear panel, individually configurable as WAN or LAN. [11] The front panel houses a USB-C console port and a multi-colour status LED. [12] The status LED indicates power, link status, and firmware-update activity through documented colour and blink patterns. [13] A reset pinhole is recessed into the rear panel beside the power input. [14] The unit supports passive PoE input on port 1 only, accepting a 24V or 48V supply. [15] An external 12V/2A power adapter is included in the box for installations that do not use PoE. [16] Wall-mount and DIN-rail brackets are sold separately. [17] The default firmware ships with the management interface bound to port 4. [18] The web management interface is accessible over HTTPS only; HTTP access is disabled by default. [19] Initial setup uses a wizard that walks the administrator through WAN configuration, admin credential creation, and timezone selection. [20] Configuration backups can be exported and imported as JSON, enabling templated rollouts across multiple sites. [21] Firmware updates are signed by Brightline's release key and verified at boot. [22] The product is certified for sale in the UK and the EU and bears the appropriate UKCA and CE marks. [23] The expected service life is seven years from first shipment, with security updates committed for that period. [24] A bulk-replacement programme is available for fleet customers approaching end of life. [25] Technical support is provided by Brightline's UK-based support team during business hours, with extended hours available under a paid support contract.

Question: What is the maximum power draw of the RT-200 router?

tier_2_judge.criteria: Answer states 18 watts (or equivalent W unit) without confusing with idle draw, operating temperature, or other specs.

metadata.notes: Easy: single spec-sheet wattage fact directly stated in production-length datasheet context; no competing wattage figures for max draw.

Context:
[1] Greenfield Hospitality manages 14 boutique hotels across the south coast and adjacent regions. [2] The chain was founded in 2008 and has grown organically and through small acquisitions in the years since. [3] Each property typically has between 25 and 60 rooms, with the average sitting at around 38 rooms per property. [4] Properties are positioned at the upper-mid market, emphasising local character over standardised brand presentation. [5] The chain was acquired by Acme Holdings in 2021 in an all-cash transaction. [6] Following the acquisition, Sara Patel was appointed as managing director, replacing the founder Tom Riley who had led the chain since its inception. [7] Patel previously ran operations for a regional restaurant group with around 40 sites across the south of England. [8] Riley remains on the board as a non-executive director and continues to advise on property acquisitions in his personal capacity. [9] Under Patel the chain has added two new properties, in Bournemouth and Hastings, and refurbished a further three. [10] The Bournemouth property opened in May 2025 with 32 rooms and has performed strongly through its first summer season. [11] The Hastings property, which opened in September 2025, has 48 rooms and a small spa. [12] Patel restructured the senior team in early 2022, creating dedicated cluster-director roles for the chain's three regional groupings. [13] The South Coast cluster comprises six properties; the West Country cluster four; and the East Anglia cluster four. [14] Each cluster director is responsible for the operational performance of their cluster and reports directly to Patel. [15] The chain's group functions, including marketing, finance, and central reservations, are based at the head office in Southampton. [16] Group-wide headcount is approximately 920 including seasonal staff. [17] Greenfield's loyalty programme, Greenfield Rewards, was relaunched under Patel's tenure with simplified tier mechanics. [18] The programme ended 2025 with approximately 84,000 active members, up 31% on the prior year. [19] Patel has spoken publicly about prioritising direct bookings over OTA channels, a strategy that has shifted the booking mix from 41% direct to 47% direct over the year. [20] The chain's 2025 group-wide occupancy averaged 78%, up from 74% in 2024. [21] Customer satisfaction scores averaged 4.4 out of 5 across the group. [22] The chain has stated no plans to expand outside the UK. [23] Acme Holdings reviews chain-level strategy with Patel quarterly. [24] The chain's annual review is signed off by the board in February each year and shared with employees in early March.

Question: Who is the current managing director of Greenfield Hospitality?

tier_2_judge.criteria: Answer names Sara Patel as the current managing director without confusing her with the predecessor Tom Riley.

metadata.notes: Easy: single directly stated 'X replaced Y' role-handover sentence in production-length company-profile context; predecessor named in same sentence is the only mild near-distractor.

Context:
[1] Acme Co's commercial terms cover billing, refunds, contract renewal, and acceptable use across all subscription plans. [2] Customers can hold either an annual or a monthly subscription. [3] Annual subscriptions are billed in advance for the full 12-month term. [4] Monthly subscriptions are billed in advance at the start of each calendar month. [5] All invoices are issued in pounds sterling unless an alternative currency has been agreed in the order form. [6] Acme Co's refund policy permits a full refund within 30 days of purchase for any annual subscription. [7] Refund requests must be submitted in writing to billing@example.com from the email address registered as the account's billing contact. [8] Refunds are processed back to the original payment method within 10 business days of approval. [9] Monthly subscriptions are non-refundable once the billing period has begun. [10] Add-on credits, once consumed, cannot be refunded; unused credits expire 12 months after purchase. [11] Annual subscriptions auto-renew on the anniversary of the original purchase unless cancelled at least 14 days in advance. [12] Customers receive a renewal reminder 45 days before the renewal date and a second reminder seven days before. [13] Cancellation can be initiated at any time via the account settings page. [14] Cancellation takes effect at the end of the current paid term. [15] Customers may downgrade an annual subscription to a lower-priced plan at renewal but not mid-term. [16] Mid-term upgrades are pro-rated against the remaining days of the current term. [17] Acme reserves the right to suspend service for accounts more than 30 days in arrears, after at least three written reminders. [18] Suspended accounts are reinstated within one business day of payment receipt. [19] Data is retained on suspended accounts for 90 days, after which deletion procedures begin. [20] Customers may export their data at any time via the account settings page in CSV or JSON format. [21] Acceptable-use policies are published separately and form part of the subscription agreement by reference. [22] Disputes are governed by the laws of England and Wales. [23] These terms were last updated in October 2025 and supersede all previous versions. [24] Material changes to the terms are notified to customers at least 30 days in advance via the in-product notification system and the billing-contact email address.

Question: How many days after purchase can an annual subscriber request a full refund?

tier_2_judge.criteria: Answer specifies 30 days clearly, optionally noting it applies to annual subscriptions; should not contradict policy details.

metadata.notes: Easy: single directly stated 30-day refund window in production-length terms context with neutral surrounding policy detail.

Context:
[1] The Northbridge Community Library serves the residents of Northbridge and the surrounding villages. [2] It is housed in a Grade II listed building on the high street, originally built as a corn exchange in the 1880s and converted to library use in the 1960s. [3] The library is open six days a week and is closed on Sundays and bank holidays. [4] Borrowing is free for residents of Northbridge District; non-residents may join for a small annual fee. [5] The collection comprises around 35,000 items, including print books, audiobooks, and a small reference section. [6] Digital lending is supported through a partnership with the regional library consortium. [7] The library hosts a children's reading hour every Saturday at 10:30 a.m. [8] Sessions run for approximately 45 minutes and are led by a member of the library's children's-services team. [9] Sessions are free but require a ticket booked in advance via the library website. [10] Each session is capped at 20 children aged 3 to 7. [11] A parent or guardian must remain on the premises throughout. [12] Refreshments are provided after the reading, and children are welcome to browse the children's section freely. [13] The reading-hour programme has run continuously since 2012, with a brief pause in 2020. [14] The library also runs a separate teen reading group for ages 13 to 16, meeting on Thursday evenings during school terms. [15] Adult programming includes a fortnightly local-history talk and a monthly book-club meeting. [16] The local-history talks are organised in partnership with the Northbridge Historical Society and frequently feature visiting speakers. [17] The library's small meeting room can be booked free of charge by community groups for non-commercial purposes. [18] Bookings are made through the library website or in person at the front desk. [19] The library has a quiet study area on the first floor with 14 desks, six of which have power sockets. [20] Free Wi-Fi is available throughout the building. [21] A small cafe operates in the entrance lobby, run by a local franchise on a long-term lease. [22] The library is staffed by a mixture of council employees and trained volunteers. [23] Volunteer applications are accepted year-round and require a short interview and a DBS check. [24] The library is supervised by a board of trustees that meets quarterly and publishes its minutes on the library's website.

Question: What is the maximum number of children allowed per reading session?

tier_2_judge.criteria: Answer states 20 children as the cap; should not conflate with the age range (3 to 7) or other numbers in the context.

metadata.notes: Easy: single directly stated session cap in production-length library-leaflet context; numbers nearby (45 minutes, ages 3 to 7) appear in the same sentence cluster but are clearly different units.

Context:
[1] The Acme Co mobile app is available on iOS and Android and provides on-the-go access to the core platform features. [2] The app was first released in 2021 and has been on a quarterly release cadence since 2023. [3] Each release is announced via in-app notification and the customer release-notes page. [4] The app shares its account system with the web platform; users sign in with the same credentials. [5] Push notifications are opt-in and configured per-user under account settings. [6] The Acme Co mobile app version 4.2 was released on 12 February 2026. [7] Version 4.2 introduced biometric login on supported devices, alongside several smaller stability improvements. [8] On iOS, biometric login uses Face ID or Touch ID where the device supports either. [9] On Android, biometric login uses the system biometric API and supports fingerprint and face unlock where available. [10] Users on Android 9 or below are no longer supported as of version 4.2; the app will continue to install but will warn the user that future updates will not be delivered. [11] iOS users on iOS 15 or above are fully supported. [12] The app supports six interface languages, with French and German added in version 4.0. [13] Localisation is handled through standard platform mechanisms and follows the device language setting by default. [14] Users may override the app language under settings. [15] The app stores recently viewed content for offline access, with a configurable cache size of up to 500 MB. [16] Cached content is encrypted at rest using the platform secure storage. [17] Network requests are made over HTTPS only and use certificate pinning to a small set of issuers. [18] Crash reporting is on by default and can be disabled in privacy settings. [19] No personally identifiable information is included in crash reports. [20] In-app purchase is not used; subscription management remains on the web account portal. [21] The app's accessibility features include support for system text scaling, high-contrast mode, and screen-reader navigation. [22] The accessibility team conducts a full audit before each major release. [23] User feedback is collected via an in-app feedback widget that captures the active screen and a short message. [24] Feedback submitted through the widget is reviewed by the product team within five business days. [25] A public roadmap covering planned mobile features for the next two quarters is maintained on the customer portal.

Question: When was version 4.2 of the Acme Co mobile app released?

tier_2_judge.criteria: Answer gives the date 12 February 2026 (or equivalent format) without speculating beyond what the context supports.

metadata.notes: Easy: single directly stated release date in production-length release-notes-style context with no competing version-release date distractors.

Context:
[1] Brightline Manufacturing operates three production lines at its Coventry plant. [2] The plant occupies a 14,000 square metre site on an industrial estate to the north of the city. [3] It has been in continuous operation since 2009 and is one of three manufacturing sites the company runs in the UK. [4] Line A produces aluminium brackets at a steady rate of 1,200 units per hour. [5] Line A has been in service since the plant opened and uses a press-and-form configuration that has been incrementally improved over time. [6] Line B, installed in 2022, produces stainless steel hinges at 800 units per hour. [7] Line B replaced an older mild-steel line that had reached the end of its serviceable life. [8] Line C produces plastic-coated fasteners and was retrofitted in early 2024 with a higher-throughput extruder. [9] Following the Line C retrofit, throughput rose from 600 units per hour to 950 units per hour. [10] The retrofit was funded as part of a planned capital programme and took the line offline for approximately three weeks. [11] The plant operates two shifts of eight hours each, six days a week. [12] All three lines share a common quality-inspection station at the end of the floor. [13] The inspection station processes a sample of one in twenty units. [14] Defective units identified at the inspection station are routed back to the originating line for rework. [15] Rework on Line A typically takes under 90 seconds per unit. [16] Rework on Line B takes longer due to the welding step involved in the hinge assembly. [17] Rework on Line C is rare and is typically handled by replacement rather than rework. [18] Plant manager Lina Okafor reports a current overall yield of 98.4%. [19] The plant supplies four major OEM customers in the West Midlands, plus a long tail of smaller accounts. [20] Customer X accounts for roughly 40% of total volume by units. [21] Customer Y, a relatively new account onboarded in late 2023, accounts for 18%. [22] Customers Z and W together account for the remaining major-customer volume, with Z larger than W. [23] The plant operates an internal continuous-improvement programme with monthly Kaizen events on each line. [24] Operator training is delivered through a structured five-week onboarding programme followed by line-specific apprenticeship rotations. [25] Health and safety performance is reviewed weekly by the plant leadership team. [26] The site has been free of lost-time incidents for 410 consecutive days as of the latest report. [27] Energy use across the plant is metered per line, supporting line-level cost attribution. [28] An on-site heat-recovery system was commissioned in late 2024 and contributes to scope 1 emissions reduction. [29] Environmental performance is reported group-wide in the annual sustainability report.

Question: What is the current production rate of Line C, and what was it before the 2024 retrofit?

expected.answer: 950 units per hour now, up from 600 units per hour before the retrofit

tier_2_judge.criteria: Answer gives both the current rate (950 units/hour) and the pre-retrofit rate (600 units/hour) for Line C, clearly attributed to the retrofit; does not conflate with Line A or Line B figures.

metadata.notes: Medium: synthesis across two adjacent sentences in production-length context, with two sibling line-rate sentences acting as mild distractors.

Context:
[1] Acme Co was founded in Manchester in 2017 as a CSV-import utility for small e-commerce stores. [2] The original product was a single-page web tool that mapped supplier price-list spreadsheets into a normalised import format. [3] In its first 18 months it acquired 220 paying customers, mostly through word of mouth in regional e-commerce communities. [4] In 2019 it raised a £500,000 angel round led by a former retail executive who had previously held senior roles at two well-known UK retailers. [5] The angel round funded the hire of two engineers and a part-time designer, alongside the company's first office lease in central Manchester. [6] By the end of 2020 the team had grown to nine, with a small support function added in mid-2020 to handle the rising volume of customer queries. [7] In early 2021 Acme launched a connector to a major payments platform, which doubled monthly signups within a quarter. [8] The connector was a notable strategic shift, marking Acme's transition from utility to platform. [9] A £4M Series A followed in November 2021, led by Brightline Ventures. [10] The Series A capital was used to expand into the US market and to build out the inventory-forecasting feature set that became the company's flagship offering. [11] By mid-2023 US revenue exceeded UK revenue for the first time. [12] The shift in revenue mix was driven primarily by faster customer acquisition in mid-market US retail accounts. [13] In 2024 the company opened an office in Austin staffed by four people, primarily handling sales and customer success for the US region. [14] A £12M Series B closed in March 2025, led by an unnamed European growth fund whose involvement is subject to a confidentiality undertaking. [15] At the time of the Series B the company had 62 employees and roughly 4,800 paying customers across the UK, EU, and US. [16] The Series B funds are earmarked for product expansion into adjacent verticals and a sales hire in Germany. [17] Acme remains majority-owned by its two co-founders, both of whom continue to hold operational roles. [18] Revenue for 2025 is reported as £18.4M, up from £11.2M the prior year. [19] The company has guided to revenue between £26M and £30M for 2026, contingent on continued strong US performance. [20] Acme's senior team includes a chief technology officer hired in 2022 and a chief revenue officer hired in 2024. [21] The board comprises the two co-founders, an investor representative from Brightline Ventures, an investor representative from the Series B lead, and two independent non-executive directors. [22] Acme's culture is described in its careers page as remote-first with optional office attendance at the Manchester and Austin sites. [23] Roughly 60% of staff work remotely from outside the two office cities. [24] The company holds an annual all-hands gathering in a different European city each year. [25] Acme has stated no plans for a near-term IPO and characterises its capital strategy as growth-oriented but cash-disciplined.

Question: Which investor led Acme Co's Series A round, and when did that round close?

expected.answer: Brightline Ventures led the Series A, which closed in November 2021

tier_2_judge.criteria: Answer correctly names Brightline Ventures and the November 2021 close date; does not confuse with the 2019 angel round or the 2025 Series B.

metadata.notes: Medium: single-sentence support but requires picking the right round among three funding events listed in the context; mild distractors include the angel round and Series B.

Context:
[1] The Acme Co API is the primary integration surface for the workflow-automation platform. [2] It is documented on the developer portal and follows REST conventions, with JSON request and response bodies. [3] All API access requires an API key, which can be created and rotated from the developer console. [4] API keys are scoped to a single tenant and may optionally be restricted to a subset of endpoints. [5] The Acme Co API rate limit on the Standard plan is 60 requests per minute per API key. [6] On the Pro plan the limit is 600 requests per minute per API key. [7] The Enterprise plan has no fixed per-minute cap but is subject to a daily quota agreed contractually. [8] Burst traffic is allowed up to 2x the per-minute limit for up to 10 seconds before throttling kicks in. [9] When the limit is exceeded, the API returns HTTP 429 with a Retry-After header. [10] The Retry-After value is given in seconds and reflects the time until the next available request slot. [11] Repeated 429 responses within a single hour may trigger a temporary 15-minute key suspension. [12] Suspensions can be appealed via the developer console. [13] Standard plan customers may request a one-time temporary uplift to the Pro limit for migration purposes. [14] Uplifts must be requested at least three business days in advance and are granted at Acme's discretion. [15] Webhook delivery attempts do not count toward the API rate limit. [16] However, webhook responses that take longer than 5 seconds are counted as failed deliveries. [17] Failed webhook deliveries are retried with exponential backoff for up to 24 hours. [18] After 24 hours of failures, the webhook subscription is automatically disabled. [19] Disabled webhooks must be re-enabled manually in the developer console. [20] All limits are documented in the Acme Co developer portal under the Reliability section. [21] The API supports both API-key authentication and OAuth 2.0 for partner integrations. [22] OAuth integrations require prior registration and review by the partnerships team. [23] All API responses include a request-id header to aid support investigations. [24] Customers should include the request-id when raising support tickets related to specific API behaviour. [25] The API offers a sandbox environment that mirrors production endpoints and uses isolated data. [26] Sandbox keys are clearly distinguished from production keys by a prefix. [27] Sandbox traffic does not count against production rate limits. [28] The platform's API change policy guarantees at least 90 days' notice before any breaking change. [29] Non-breaking changes, including additive changes to response payloads, may be introduced without notice. [30] A monthly developer newsletter summarises notable API changes and upcoming deprecations.

Question: What HTTP status code does the API return when the rate limit is exceeded, and what header tells the client when to retry?

expected.answer: HTTP 429 with a Retry-After header indicating the wait time in seconds

tier_2_judge.criteria: Answer correctly identifies HTTP 429 and the Retry-After header, optionally noting the seconds unit; does not invent additional headers or codes.

metadata.notes: Medium: two-sentence synthesis (status code + header semantics) in production-length developer-docs context; the request-id header sentence later in the context acts as a mild distractor.

Context:
[1] Greenfield Hospitality's loyalty programme, Greenfield Rewards, has three tiers: Silver, Gold, and Platinum. [2] The programme was relaunched in 2022 with simplified mechanics and ended 2025 with around 84,000 active members. [3] Silver is the entry tier, granted after a member's first paid stay at any Greenfield property. [4] Gold is granted at 10 paid nights within a rolling 12-month window. [5] Platinum is granted at 30 paid nights within a rolling 12-month window. [6] Silver members receive a 5% discount on direct bookings made through the Greenfield website or app. [7] Gold members receive a 10% discount on direct bookings and one complimentary room upgrade per stay, subject to availability at check-in. [8] Platinum members receive a 15% discount, guaranteed late checkout to 3 p.m., and a welcome amenity at every property. [9] Members at all tiers earn 1 point per £1 spent on accommodation, excluding taxes and fees. [10] Spend on food and beverage at on-site restaurants also earns points, at 0.5 points per £1. [11] Points expire 18 months after the most recent earning activity. [12] 1,000 points can be redeemed for a £10 statement credit on a future booking. [13] Points cannot be redeemed against third-party bookings made via OTAs. [14] Tier status is reviewed monthly. [15] If a member falls below the threshold for their current tier, they retain the tier for the remainder of the calendar year before being downgraded. [16] Children under 16 staying in the same room as a member do not contribute to night counts. [17] Members can pause their account for up to six months, during which time tier expiry is also paused. [18] Pauses must be requested in advance via the loyalty portal and cannot be applied retroactively. [19] During a pause, members continue to receive marketing communications unless they unsubscribe. [20] The programme partners with a small number of regional restaurants and attractions, where members can earn points by paying with a registered card. [21] Partner-earned points are credited to the member's account within 14 days of the qualifying transaction. [22] The full list of partners is published on the loyalty portal and is updated quarterly. [23] Members may share points with one nominated household member, subject to verification of address. [24] Points sharing must be set up in advance and cannot be reversed within 30 days of activation. [25] Disputes about point balances should be raised with the loyalty team within 60 days of the relevant stay. [26] The programme's terms and conditions are reviewed annually and were last updated in November 2025. [27] Material changes to the terms are notified to active members at least 30 days in advance via email.

Question: What benefit, in addition to a discount, does the Gold tier provide that Silver does not?

expected.answer: One complimentary room upgrade per stay, subject to availability

tier_2_judge.criteria: Answer names the complimentary room upgrade benefit (with or without the availability caveat); does not list Platinum-only benefits as Gold benefits.

metadata.notes: Medium: single-sentence support requires distinguishing Gold-only benefits from the immediately following Platinum benefits sentence.

Context:
[1] The Northbridge Council leisure centre is operated by the local authority and serves the residents of Northbridge District. [2] The centre comprises a 25-metre swimming pool, a gym with around 80 stations, two squash courts, a sauna and steam room, and three multi-use studios. [3] The centre is open seven days a week, with extended hours on weekdays. [4] Membership entitles holders to access the facilities included in their tier across all opening hours, subject to peak-time limits in some cases. [5] The centre offers four membership types: Off-Peak, Standard, Family, and Concession. [6] Off-Peak membership costs £24 per month and grants access between 10 a.m. and 4 p.m. on weekdays only. [7] Standard membership costs £38 per month and grants access during all opening hours, seven days a week. [8] Family membership costs £62 per month, covers up to two adults and three children under 16, and includes free swimming lessons for the children. [9] Concession membership is available to over-65s, full-time students, and registered carers, at £18 per month with the same access as Standard. [10] All membership types include access to the gym, swimming pool, and sauna. [11] Group fitness classes carry a £4 drop-in fee for Off-Peak members and are included for all other tiers. [12] A £15 joining fee applies to all new members and is waived during the January promotional period. [13] Members may freeze their membership for up to three months in any 12-month period at a £5 monthly admin fee. [14] Cancellation requires 30 days' written notice and may be submitted in person at reception or by email. [15] Refunds for unused full months are issued only if cancellation is due to relocation or medical reasons, with supporting documentation. [16] Day passes are sold at reception for £8 (adult) or £4 (child) and grant access to the gym and pool but not group classes. [17] Day-pass holders may upgrade to include classes for an additional £4. [18] The centre is closed on Christmas Day and Boxing Day. [19] On New Year's Day the centre opens from noon to 6 p.m. [20] All members receive a 10% discount at the on-site cafe. [21] The cafe is operated by a local franchise and serves hot meals, snacks, and drinks. [22] Bookings for swim lanes can be made via the centre's mobile app up to seven days in advance. [23] Squash court bookings can be made up to five days in advance and are subject to a small per-hour court fee. [24] Personal training sessions are available with the centre's qualified trainers and are billed separately from membership. [25] The centre runs structured swim courses for adults and children during school terms. [26] Course fees vary by length and are published on the centre's website. [27] Lifeguards are on duty during all swimming pool sessions. [28] CCTV operates throughout the public areas of the centre, excluding changing rooms.

Question: How much does Concession membership cost per month, and which groups are eligible?

expected.answer: £18 per month; eligible groups are over-65s, full-time students, and registered carers

tier_2_judge.criteria: Answer states £18 per month and lists all three eligible groups (over-65s, full-time students, registered carers); does not include ineligible groups.

metadata.notes: Medium: single-sentence support requiring extraction of multiple coordinated facts (price + three eligibility groups) from a context with several other membership-tier price distractors.

Context:
[1] Acme Co's data retention policy describes how customer data is held, deleted, and protected throughout its lifecycle. [2] The policy applies to all customers on all subscription plans and is binding on all Acme staff with access to production systems. [3] Acme categorises customer data into three classes: transactional, behavioural, and support. [4] Transactional data includes invoices, payment records, and contract documents. [5] Transactional data is retained for seven years after account closure to satisfy statutory accounting obligations. [6] Behavioural data includes product usage logs, feature interaction events, and session metadata. [7] Behavioural data is retained for 18 months after last account activity, then aggregated and anonymised for analytics. [8] Aggregated behavioural data is retained indefinitely and contains no individual identifiers. [9] Support data includes ticket transcripts, email correspondence, and call recordings where applicable. [10] Support data is retained for three years after ticket closure. [11] Customers may request earlier deletion of behavioural and support data via a verified written request to privacy@example.com. [12] Verified requests are actioned within 30 calendar days of receipt. [13] Transactional data cannot be deleted earlier than the statutory window. [14] Backups containing any of the three classes are rotated on a 90-day cycle. [15] Data appearing only in backups is purged within 90 days of the primary deletion. [16] All data, regardless of class, is encrypted at rest using AES-256. [17] Data in transit is protected with TLS 1.3 or above. [18] Access to production data is logged and reviewed quarterly by the security team. [19] Production-data access is restricted to a small named group of engineers who have completed Acme's security training. [20] Access reviews follow a least-privilege principle and may result in revocation of unused entitlements. [21] The retention policy is reviewed annually and was last updated in October 2025. [22] Customers will be notified at least 30 days before any material change to the retention windows takes effect. [23] Acme maintains a public sub-processor list, which is updated whenever a sub-processor is added or removed. [24] Customers may subscribe to sub-processor change notifications via the trust portal. [25] Acme is registered with the UK Information Commissioner's Office as a data controller and as a data processor for customer-controlled data. [26] Data subject access requests received by Acme on behalf of customer data are forwarded to the relevant customer for handling within five business days. [27] Acme cooperates with customers on data-subject responses and provides tools to support customer-side handling. [28] Where customer data is accessed under lawful order, Acme will, where legally permissible, notify the affected customer.

Question: How long is behavioural data retained, and what happens to it after that period?

expected.answer: 18 months after last account activity, then aggregated and anonymised for analytics

tier_2_judge.criteria: Answer gives the 18-month retention period AND describes the post-period treatment (aggregation and anonymisation); does not confuse with transactional or support retention windows.

metadata.notes: Medium: single-sentence support requires extracting both a duration and a follow-on action; transactional and support retention windows act as parallel-shape distractors.

[1] Brightline Ventures' 2025 portfolio review covered 42 active investments across three funds. [2] The firm publishes an internal portfolio review annually for limited partners and the firm's investment committee. [3] Fund I, vintage 2017, holds 14 companies and is now in its harvest phase. [4] Fund II, vintage 2020, holds 19 companies and is mid-life. [5] Fund III, vintage 2023, holds nine companies and is still actively deploying. [6] Across all three funds, the median company stage at first investment was Series A. [7] The largest single check written from Fund III to date was £8.2M into a logistics-software company based in Bristol. [8] Fund II had three exits in 2024: an acquisition of a SaaS analytics company at 4.1x cost, a secondary sale at 1.8x cost, and a write-off. [9] Fund I had no exits in 2024 but two of its remaining holdings filed for IPO in early 2025. [10] The firm's gross MOIC across all funds is reported at 2.3x. [11] Net IRR for Fund I stands at 19% as of year-end 2025. [12] Fund II's interim net IRR is 14%, reflecting earlier-stage holdings that are yet to mark up substantially. [13] Fund III is too early to report meaningful IRR. [14] Brightline's investment team consists of four partners and seven associates. [15] In 2025 the firm hired two new associates focused on climate-tech sourcing. [16] The firm operates from offices in London and Edinburgh. [17] Annual letters to LPs are sent in March and September. [18] The mid-year letter focuses on portfolio company progress and notable new investments. [19] The annual letter includes audited financials and a forward-looking commentary from the managing partner. [20] The firm follows a thesis-driven approach focused on B2B software, climate tech, and selected fintech opportunities. [21] Sourcing is split roughly evenly between inbound referrals and proactive outbound work led by the associates. [22] The firm maintains a published anti-portfolio of notable passes, updated annually. [23] Brightline is a signatory to the relevant industry codes on responsible investment. [24] Annual ESG reporting is integrated into the September letter and follows the standard industry framework. [25] The firm's relationship with its LPs is described as long-tenured, with most current LPs having backed at least two of the three funds. [26] Fundraising for Fund IV is expected to begin in late 2026, subject to deployment progress on Fund III. [27] The firm has a defined succession plan for the senior partnership, last reviewed in 2024. [28] No partner departures occurred in 2025. [29] The firm's compliance function is outsourced to a specialist provider and is reviewed by an internal compliance lead.

Question: How many exits did Fund II have in 2024, and what was the multiple on the highest-performing exit?

Context:
[1] Acme Co operates two distinct cloud regions: EU-West (Dublin) and UK-South (London). [2] EU-West was the company's original region, launched in 2019. [3] UK-South was launched in late 2022 in response to customer demand for data residency within the United Kingdom. [4] By default, new customers in the United Kingdom are provisioned into UK-South. [5] Customers signing up from elsewhere in Europe default to EU-West. [6] Customers may request a region change at any time, but data is not automatically migrated. [7] Region migration is offered as a paid professional service, typically priced between £4,000 and £12,000 depending on data volume. [8] Inter-region replication is available on Enterprise plans only. [9] When inter-region replication is enabled, writes are committed in the primary region and asynchronously replicated to the secondary region within 30 seconds under normal conditions. [10] Replication latency may rise transiently during peak load periods but should normally remain within the stated 30-second window. [11] In the event of a primary-region outage, failover to the secondary region is initiated manually by Acme's on-call engineering team. [12] Failover is not automatic, by deliberate design, in order to allow human assessment of the outage character before redirecting traffic. [13] Manual failover targets a recovery time objective of 15 minutes from the moment failover is triggered. [14] The recovery point objective for replicated data is 60 seconds. [15] Customers without inter-region replication enabled are subject to the single-region uptime SLA of 99.9%. [16] Customers with inter-region replication enabled are subject to a multi-region SLA of 99.99%. [17] Both SLAs exclude scheduled maintenance windows announced at least 72 hours in advance. [18] Scheduled maintenance is typically performed between 02:00 and 04:00 UTC on the first Sunday of each month. [19] In 2024 there were two unplanned outages affecting EU-West, both lasting under 20 minutes. [20] UK-South had no unplanned outages in 2024. [21] Both regions are independently audited annually for SOC 2 Type II compliance. [22] The most recent audit was completed in November 2025, with the report available to customers under NDA. [23] Customers may request the executive summary of the audit report through the trust portal. [24] Acme also maintains a status page at status.example.com which posts updates within 10 minutes of incident detection. [25] Status updates include initial detection, investigation, mitigation, and resolution phases, plus a post-incident summary. [26] Historical uptime data for both regions is published quarterly. [27] The Q4 2025 reports show 99.97% uptime for EU-West and 99.99% for UK-South. [28] Latency-sensitive customers may use the Acme edge cache, which has 14 points of presence across Europe. [29] The edge cache is included on the Pro plan and above. [30] Edge cache hit ratios are reported in the customer dashboard, refreshed hourly. [31] The cache may be configured per-tenant with custom TTLs and invalidation rules. [32] Cache invalidation requests propagate across all points of presence within two minutes. [33] Each region operates from at least three independent availability zones, with workloads distributed across zones automatically. [34] Single-zone failures within a region are handled automatically with no customer action required and no observable downtime in the typical case. [35] Customers may opt into a backup-only secondary region, distinct from full inter-region replication, in which daily encrypted backups are stored in the alternate region for disaster recovery purposes. [36] Backup-only restores have a separately documented RTO measured in hours rather than minutes. [37] All region-related contractual commitments are documented in the master service agreement and supplementary regional addenda.

Question: What is the recovery time objective for a manual failover between regions, and what is the corresponding recovery point objective?

expected.answer: RTO is 15 minutes from failover trigger; RPO for replicated data is 60 seconds

tier_2_judge.criteria: Answer correctly identifies the 15-minute RTO and 60-second RPO, ideally labelling each; should not be confused with the 30-second replication latency, the SLAs, the backup-only RTO, or the 10-minute status update timing.

metadata.notes: Hard: production-length context with sharper distractors (30-second replication, 99.9%/99.99% SLAs, 10-minute status updates, hours-scale backup-only RTO) competing with the answer; requires citing two adjacent sentences and disambiguating manual failover from backup-only restore.

Context:
[1] The Brightline 2025 Sustainability Report covers operations across the company's three UK sites: Leeds (head office and primary depot), Sheffield (secondary depot), and Coventry (manufacturing plant). [2] The report follows the GHG Protocol corporate accounting standard for emissions and is published alongside the company's annual financial accounts. [3] Total scope 1 emissions across all sites in 2025 were 4,820 tonnes CO2e, down from 5,310 tonnes in 2024. [4] Scope 2 emissions, market-based, were 1,140 tonnes CO2e, down from 1,690 tonnes in 2024. [5] Scope 3 emissions, primarily from upstream raw-material transport, were estimated at 9,250 tonnes CO2e, broadly flat year on year. [6] The Leeds site contributed 38% of scope 1 emissions, the Sheffield site 22%, and the Coventry plant 40%. [7] Scope 2 reductions were driven primarily by the Coventry plant transitioning to a renewable electricity tariff in March 2025. [8] The Leeds site is targeting a similar transition in Q3 2026. [9] The Sheffield site already procures renewable electricity under a 2023 contract. [10] In 2025 Brightline replaced 28 diesel vans with electric equivalents at the Leeds depot. [11] An additional 40 electric vans are budgeted for 2026, with deliveries phased across the year. [12] The current fleet stands at 240 vans, of which 52 are now electric. [13] The Sheffield depot has 80 vans, 6 of which are electric. [14] Electric van charging at Leeds is supported by a 22-bay onsite charger array installed in 2024. [15] The charger array is fed from the depot's grid connection and supplemented by a small rooftop solar installation completed in mid-2025. [16] The Coventry plant's heat-recovery system, commissioned in late 2024, recovered an estimated 1.2 GWh of usable heat in 2025. [17] This heat-recovery system is the single largest contributor to the year-on-year scope 1 reduction at Coventry. [18] Process-improvement work on Line C, undertaken alongside the early-2024 retrofit, contributed a smaller secondary reduction in scope 1 emissions at Coventry through reduced fuel use in the extruder pre-heat stage. [19] Water usage across all three sites was 38,400 cubic metres in 2025, a 4% reduction on 2024. [20] Waste sent to landfill fell to 12 tonnes from 28 tonnes the prior year, a 57% reduction. [21] Recycling rates across the three sites averaged 84% in 2025, up from 79% in 2024. [22] The company's stated 2030 target is a 50% reduction in scope 1 emissions versus a 2022 baseline of 6,100 tonnes. [23] As of 2025 the company is approximately 21% of the way to that target. [24] No specific scope 3 reduction target has yet been set, pending completion of a full upstream supplier audit due in mid-2026. [25] The supplier audit is expected to inform a target-setting exercise in the second half of 2026. [26] The report was independently assured by an external auditor in February 2026. [27] The auditor flagged moderate uncertainty in the scope 3 estimate due to incomplete supplier data. [28] All numerical figures in the report are rounded to the nearest unit shown. [29] The full report is available on Brightline's investor relations site. [30] The 2026 report is scheduled for publication in February 2027. [31] Annual emissions data is also submitted to the CDP under the standard reporting framework. [32] Brightline became a CDP reporter in 2021. [33] Its current CDP score is B, up from C in 2023. [34] The company's sustainability strategy is overseen by a board sub-committee chaired by one of the non-executive directors. [35] Operational responsibility sits with the head of sustainability, who joined the company in 2022 and reports to the chief operating officer. [36] The sustainability team consists of four people based primarily at the Leeds head office.

Question: What was the single largest driver of Brightline's year-on-year scope 1 reduction at the Coventry plant in 2025?

expected.answer: The heat-recovery system commissioned in late 2024, which recovered an estimated 1.2 GWh of usable heat in 2025

tier_2_judge.criteria: Answer identifies the heat-recovery system at Coventry as the largest driver of scope 1 reduction; should not confuse it with the renewable electricity tariff (which drove scope 2), the Line C process work (a smaller secondary contributor), or the electric van replacements.

metadata.notes: Hard: production-length sustainability context with multiple plausible-sounding climate initiatives as distractors (renewable tariff, EVs, Line C process work explicitly described as a smaller secondary contributor); requires distinguishing scope 1 from scope 2 drivers.

Context:
[1] The Northbridge District Hospital Trust operates four hospitals: Northbridge General, Northbridge South, Westgate Community, and Eastview Specialist. [2] Northbridge General is the largest with 620 inpatient beds and a 24/7 emergency department. [3] Northbridge South has 340 beds and a minor injuries unit but no full A&E. [4] Westgate Community has 180 beds, focused on rehabilitation and elderly care. [5] Eastview Specialist has 220 beds and is the regional centre for cardiothoracic surgery. [6] In the financial year 2024-25 the trust admitted 142,000 inpatients across all four sites. [7] Outpatient appointments numbered 980,000 in the same period. [8] Average length of stay across the trust was 4.6 days, slightly below the national benchmark of 4.9 days. [9] Northbridge General had the highest occupancy at 94%. [10] Westgate Community had the lowest occupancy at 81%. [11] The trust employs approximately 9,400 staff, of whom 1,100 are doctors and 3,800 are nurses. [12] Staff turnover in 2024-25 was 11.2%, broadly in line with the regional average. [13] The trust reported a deficit of £18.4M for the year, against a planned deficit of £12M. [14] The overspend is attributed mainly to higher-than-anticipated agency staffing costs at Northbridge General and Eastview. [15] Agency reliance has been a persistent challenge for the trust, particularly in nursing roles in the cardiothoracic and emergency specialties. [16] A cost-improvement programme has been initiated targeting £22M of recurrent savings over two years. [17] The CIP includes consolidation of pathology services across the four sites into a single hub at Northbridge General. [18] The pathology consolidation is scheduled to complete by the end of 2026 and is forecast to deliver around £4M of recurrent savings once steady state is reached. [19] A separate workstream within the CIP focuses on reducing reliance on agency staffing through targeted permanent recruitment and an internal staff bank. [20] The internal staff bank, launched in early 2025, has filled around 8% of shifts that would previously have gone to external agencies. [21] Patient satisfaction scores averaged 84% across the trust, with Westgate Community scoring highest at 91% and Eastview lowest at 78%. [22] The Eastview score reflects, in part, longer average waiting times for elective cardiothoracic procedures. [23] Mean waiting time for elective surgery at Eastview was 18 weeks, against a national target of 14 weeks. [24] Mean waiting time at Northbridge General was 11 weeks. [25] Cancer 62-day pathway compliance was 78% across the trust, against a 85% national standard. [26] A&E four-hour performance at Northbridge General was 71%, against a 95% national standard. [27] The trust has invested £6.8M in a new electronic patient record system, due to go live at all four sites in spring 2026. [28] The EPR investment is funded through national capital allocation rather than the trust's recurrent budget. [29] An additional £2.1M has been earmarked for refurbishment of the Westgate Community rehabilitation wards. [30] Capital expenditure for the year totalled £14.3M. [31] The trust's chair, appointed in 2023, will step down at the end of 2026. [32] Recruitment for the chair's successor will begin in summer 2026. [33] Board meetings are held publicly on the second Thursday of each month. [34] Minutes are published within 10 working days. [35] The trust publishes an annual quality account separately from the annual report. [36] The annual quality account focuses on clinical outcomes, patient experience, and quality-improvement initiatives. [37] The trust's annual report was approved by the board in March 2026 and laid before parliament shortly thereafter.

Question: To what was the trust's £18.4M deficit primarily attributed?

expected.answer: Higher-than-anticipated agency staffing costs at Northbridge General and Eastview

tier_2_judge.criteria: Answer identifies agency staffing costs (at Northbridge General and Eastview) as the primary driver of the deficit; should not be misled by the pathology consolidation, EPR investment, capital expenditure figures, or the staff-bank initiative.

metadata.notes: Hard: production-length financial/operational context where multiple cost-related items appear (CIP, EPR investment funded by capital allocation, refurbishment, capex, staff-bank progress) as distractors for the deficit-cause question.

Context:
[1] Acme Co's incident postmortem for the 14 March 2026 outage covers a 47-minute service degradation in the EU-West region. [2] The incident affected the platform's primary API surface and several dependent internal services. [3] The incident began at 09:18 UTC when API error rates rose from a baseline of 0.04% to 12% within three minutes. [4] On-call engineers were paged at 09:21 UTC by the automated alerting system. [5] Initial investigation focused on a recently deployed change to the rate-limiter service. [6] That change had been deployed at 08:55 UTC and was the first hypothesis on the basis of timing alone. [7] The rate-limiter change had touched code paths exercised by every API request, which made it a plausible candidate. [8] However, by 09:34 UTC, log analysis ruled out the rate-limiter change as the cause: error patterns did not match the new code path, and a controlled rollback in the staging environment did not reproduce the issue. [9] Attention shifted to the upstream message broker, which was showing elevated consumer lag. [10] Initial broker telemetry suggested possible queue backpressure but did not, on closer inspection, account for the full error pattern observed at the API edge. [11] At 09:41 UTC the on-call team identified the root cause as a misconfigured connection pool size on a database proxy that had been quietly degrading for six hours, finally tipping into saturation. [12] The misconfiguration originated from a routine config push at 03:00 UTC that morning, which set the pool size to 50 instead of the intended 500. [13] The discrepancy went unnoticed because the proxy continued to serve requests, just at increasing latency, until peak morning traffic exceeded available connections. [14] Once identified, the fix was straightforward: the pool size was corrected and the proxy restarted. [15] Service was fully restored by 10:05 UTC. [16] The postmortem identifies three contributing factors. [17] First, the config push at 03:00 UTC bypassed the usual peer-review process under an emergency change ticket that was, on review, not actually urgent. [18] Second, the alerting threshold for connection-pool saturation was set at 95% utilisation, which proved too high to give meaningful early warning. [19] Third, the dashboard that would have shown the gradually rising latency was not part of the on-call team's standard morning checks. [20] Customer impact was significant but bounded: roughly 2,400 customers experienced elevated error rates, of whom 380 reported issues via the support channel. [21] No data loss occurred. [22] No SLA credits were automatically triggered, as the regional uptime for March remained above the 99.9% threshold. [23] Three customers on enterprise contracts have requested goodwill credits, which are being evaluated case by case. [24] Action items from the postmortem include: tightening the emergency change process to require a second approver in all cases, lowering the saturation alert threshold to 75%, and adding the latency dashboard to the morning checklist. [25] A further action item commits to a quarterly review of all configuration parameters with non-default values on production database proxies. [26] All action items are owned by the platform engineering team with target completion by end of April 2026. [27] A follow-up review is scheduled for May 2026 to confirm the action items have been completed and tested. [28] The postmortem was authored by the on-call lead and reviewed by the head of engineering. [29] It was published internally on 17 March 2026 and shared with affected enterprise customers under NDA on 19 March 2026. [30] Acme's standard postmortem template was followed throughout, including the explicit blameless framing required by the engineering handbook. [31] The handbook also requires that each postmortem be referenced in subsequent quarterly reliability reviews until all action items have been demonstrably closed. [32] The reliability review for Q2 2026 is scheduled for early July and will include this incident as a standing agenda item.

Question: What was the actual root cause of the 14 March 2026 outage, and how long had the underlying misconfiguration been silently degrading service before the incident was detected?

expected.answer: A misconfigured connection pool size (50 instead of 500) on a database proxy, which had been silently degrading for six hours before saturation

tier_2_judge.criteria: Answer correctly identifies the misconfigured database proxy connection pool (ideally noting 50 vs 500) as the root cause and the six-hour silent-degradation duration; should not name the rate-limiter change (which the postmortem explicitly ruled out) or the message broker (which was investigated but did not account for the error pattern).

metadata.notes: Hard (preserved design): postmortem narrative includes a discarded initial hypothesis (rate-limiter) plus a secondary investigative dead-end (message broker) as deliberate distractors; tests careful disambiguation between provisional and confirmed cause across a longer narrative.

The sentence indices match the [N] markers in the context. If the context does not support an answer, set answer to "unknown" and supporting_sentences to []. Do not include any other text.

Context:
[1] The Greenfield Hospitality 2025 annual review describes performance across the chain's 14 boutique hotels. [2] The chain is divided into three regional clusters: South Coast (six properties), West Country (four properties), and East Anglia (four properties). [3] Total room revenue for 2025 was £52.1M, up 7.8% on 2024. [4] Food and beverage revenue was £18.4M, broadly flat year on year. [5] Other revenue, including spa, events, and parking, was £6.2M, up 12% on 2024. [6] Group-wide occupancy averaged 78%, up from 74% in 2024. [7] The South Coast cluster led on occupancy at 84%, with the East Anglia cluster lowest at 71%. [8] The West Country cluster sat at 79% occupancy. [9] Average daily rate (ADR) across the group was £142, up £6 on the prior year. [10] The South Coast cluster commanded the highest ADR at £158, reflecting strong summer demand. [11] The East Anglia cluster's ADR was £128, the lowest in the group. [12] RevPAR figures broadly mirror the occupancy and ADR patterns, with the South Coast cluster leading and East Anglia trailing. [13] The standout property of the year on commercial metrics was the Bournemouth Bay Hotel, which posted an 89% occupancy and a £176 ADR. [14] The Bournemouth Bay Hotel is part of the South Coast cluster and benefits from a strong location on the seafront. [15] Two new properties opened during 2025: a 32-room property in Hastings in May and a 48-room property in Cornwall in September. [16] Both new properties are included in the figures above on a part-year basis. [17] The Hastings property reached 76% occupancy by Q4, exceeding initial projections. [18] The Cornwall property is too early to assess but tracked at 64% occupancy in its first full quarter. [19] Two older properties underwent refurbishment during the year: a four-week closure of the Lyme Regis property in February and a six-week closure of the Norwich property in October-November. [20] Capex for 2025 was £8.9M, of which £5.4M related to the two refurbishments. [21] Staff headcount across the group is approximately 920, including seasonal staff peaking in July and August. [22] Staff turnover was 22%, down from 27% in 2024. [23] Customer satisfaction scores averaged 4.4 out of 5 across the group. [24] The Bournemouth Bay Hotel topped customer satisfaction at 4.7. [25] The Norwich property scored lowest at 3.9, reflecting disruption during refurbishment. [26] The Lyme Regis property's satisfaction score recovered to 4.5 in the months following its February refurbishment, having dipped during the closure period. [27] The Cornwall property scored 4.3 in its first full quarter, broadly in line with group average. [28] Loyalty programme sign-ups grew by 31% over the year, ending 2025 with approximately 84,000 active members. [29] Direct bookings as a share of total bookings rose from 41% to 47%, reducing reliance on OTA channels. [30] OTA commission costs fell by £620,000 as a result. [31] Marketing spend was largely held flat year on year, with a small reallocation toward direct-channel performance marketing. [32] The chain plans no new openings in 2026 but is exploring two acquisition targets in the Lake District. [33] The 2026 capex budget is £6.2M, focused on guest-room technology upgrades and a small extension to the Bournemouth property's restaurant capacity. [34] The chain's central reservations team handled 38% more direct booking enquiries in 2025 than the prior year, supported by additional headcount added in Q2. [35] The annual review was signed off by the board in February 2026 and discussed at the half-year LP investor briefing in March.

Question: Which property recorded the highest customer satisfaction score in 2025, and what was the score?

expected.answer: The Bournemouth Bay Hotel, with a score of 4.7 out of 5

tier_2_judge.criteria: Answer names the Bournemouth Bay Hotel and gives the 4.7 score; should not confuse with occupancy/ADR figures (where Bournemouth also leads), the lowest-scoring Norwich property, or the Lyme Regis post-refurbishment recovery score.

metadata.notes: Hard: production-length context where the same property leads on multiple commercial metrics (occupancy, ADR, satisfaction) plus several other property-level satisfaction figures (Norwich, Lyme Regis, Cornwall) as distractors; tests precise metric-to-fact matching.

Context:
[1] Acme Co's product security advisory ASA-2026-003 concerns a vulnerability in the Acme Connector library, versions 2.4.0 through 2.6.1 inclusive. [2] The advisory was published as part of Acme's coordinated disclosure programme and is the third such advisory issued in 2026. [3] The vulnerability allows an authenticated user with the "reader" role to escalate privileges to "editor" by crafting a specific sequence of API requests against the connector's permission-cache endpoint. [4] The CVSS v3.1 base score is 7.5, classified as High severity. [5] The vector reflects the requirement for an authenticated session and the high integrity impact of the privilege escalation. [6] The vulnerability does not affect users on connector versions 2.3.x or earlier, nor versions 2.6.2 and later. [7] Versions in the 2.3.x line did not include the permission-cache endpoint, which was first introduced in 2.4.0. [8] Versions 2.6.2 and later include the corrective change. [9] Exploitation requires the attacker to already hold a valid reader-role API token for the target tenant. [10] Anonymous exploitation is not possible. [11] No known exploitation in the wild has been observed at the time of advisory publication. [12] A patched release, version 2.6.2, is available immediately. [13] Customers using the Acme-managed cloud connector were automatically patched on 22 April 2026; no action is required for managed-cloud customers. [14] Customers running the self-hosted connector should upgrade to 2.6.2 or later as soon as practicable. [15] As a temporary mitigation for self-hosted users unable to upgrade immediately, the permission-cache endpoint can be disabled by setting the configuration flag PERMISSION_CACHE_ENABLED to false; this disables the vulnerable code path at the cost of slightly increased authorisation latency. [16] Customers who choose the configuration mitigation should still plan to upgrade within 30 days. [17] The vulnerability was responsibly disclosed by an external security researcher on 8 April 2026. [18] Acme's product security team confirmed the issue on 9 April 2026 and developed a patch by 18 April 2026. [19] The patch underwent internal review and regression testing between 18 and 21 April 2026. [20] Public advisory was published on 24 April 2026, two business days after the patched release was made available, in line with Acme's coordinated disclosure policy. [21] Acme has assigned the identifier CVE-2026-12345 to this vulnerability. [22] Customers may verify their connector version by querying the /version endpoint, which returns the running version string. [23] Customers using infrastructure-as-code tooling should update their connector image references to pull the 2.6.2 tag or later. [24] Acme strongly recommends rotating any reader-role API tokens that may have been exposed to untrusted parties, even though no exploitation has been observed. [25] Customers concerned about historical exploitation may request log analysis from Acme support, who will look for the specific request signature associated with the vulnerability. [26] Log retention for connector audit logs is 90 days for managed-cloud customers and depends on local configuration for self-hosted customers. [27] A separate, lower-severity advisory ASA-2026-002, published in March 2026, affected versions 2.5.0 through 2.5.4 of the connector and is unrelated to the issue described here despite the overlapping version range. [28] The advisory will be updated if material new information emerges. [29] Subscribers to the security-advisories mailing list received the advisory via email on the publication date. [30] Customers who wish to subscribe can do so via their account preferences. [31] Acme thanks the reporting researcher for the responsible disclosure and has acknowledged them in the advisory's credits section. [32] A bounty payment was made under Acme's standard vulnerability disclosure programme. [33] The amount of the bounty is confidential. [34] Acme conducts an annual review of its disclosure policy, with the next review due in October 2026. [35] Customers with questions on the advisory can contact security@example.com.

Question: Which exact versions of the Acme Connector library are affected by the vulnerability, and which versions are not affected?

expected.answer: Affected: versions 2.4.0 through 2.6.1 inclusive. Not affected: versions 2.3.x or earlier, and versions 2.6.2 or later

tier_2_judge.criteria: Answer correctly states the affected range (2.4.0 to 2.6.1 inclusive) AND both unaffected ranges (2.3.x or earlier, and 2.6.2 or later); should not omit either side of the unaffected range, conflate with the unrelated ASA-2026-002 advisory's 2.5.0-2.5.4 range, or include incorrect versions.

metadata.notes: Hard: production-length security-advisory context with multiple version-related distractors (patched release tag, IaC image reference, /version endpoint, and a deliberately overlapping unrelated prior advisory ASA-2026-002 covering 2.5.0-2.5.4); requires precise extraction of an inclusive range plus both exclusion boundaries from two non-adjacent sentences while disregarding the unrelated advisory.
